steps.text: Rinse sample lines with methanol, air dry, and then purge at the probe maximum temperature with nitrogen or zero air for a minimum of one hour.
Heat PFA sample lines to the maximum operating temperature (≥150 °C/300 °F) and flush with nitrogen or zero air for approximately two hours.
Heat and flush sample lines used for previous sampling as in Section 8.4.3.1 before re-use.
Rinse highly contaminated lines with methanol then heat and flush as in Section 8.4.3.1.
Use new PFA sample tubing inside a larger diameter heated tube for replacement after each use.
Determine which sampling system configuration is appropriate for sampling (Section 8.1, 8.2, or 8.3).
Determine stack gas moisture content.
For combustion sources, assume moisture content is >3% v/v and water/acid gas management is required (Section 8.2).
For non-combustion sources, determine if acid gases are suspected or present based on process knowledge.
Use the VFC Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management for gases where moisture is >3% v/v and/or acid gases are present.
Use the Direct VFC Sampling System for gases where moisture is confirmed to be ≤3% v/v and acid gases are not suspected.
Prepare the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid-Gas Management impinger conditioning system.
Pour approximately 15 mL of ultrapure DI water into each of the first three impingers (about 1/3 full).
Place an empty knockout impinger in the fourth position.
Place the impingers in an ice bath.
Connect the fourth impinger to the tee with the sampling orifice using PFA or stainless-steel tubing.
Prepare the Direct VFC Sampling System.
Connect the sample probe liner to the heated glass filter housing with a QFF or GFF.
Connect the exit of the heated glass filter housing to the impinger conditioning system if water/acid gas management is needed.
Connect the appropriate transfer line in series with the critical orifice tee and the tubing to the bypass pump.
Open the canister valve, record the start time/start timer, and collect the sample for approximately 60 minutes.
Collect the sample gas at a minimum of at least three discreet duct or stack traverse points, sampling for an equal amount of time at each traverse point.
When the run is complete, close the canister valve, record the stop time, perform a post-test leak check, and disconnect the canister from the train.
Confirm the final canister vacuum.
Attach the vacuum gauge to the canister and open the canister valve.
Record the final pressure.

Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume to the theoretical volume sampled from the sample time and orifice flow rate. A RPD > 20% indicates a potential leak or a clogged orifice and requires resampling.
Close the valve, remove the gauge, and replace the canister inlet cap.
Complete the COC sheets, make copies, and place the originals in the shipping boxes.
Once the canisters have been sampled, place them in their individual protective containers and then in a shipping box.
Store canisters in a dry location at ambient laboratory temperature.
Ship canisters to laboratory for analysis.
Upon receipt of the canisters at the laboratory, measure and record the canister pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume with the theoretical volume sampled in the field from the sample time and orifice flow rate provided on the COC.
Samples must be analyzed within 30 days.
Determine the sampling system background.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect the sample as described in Section 8.6. Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister.
Submit the canister.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.
Check the field integrity of collected samples.
Perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8.
Flag data that differ between the two approaches by ≥20% RPD and indicate if leakage is suspected and invalidate the sample with a similar explanation.
Check the laboratory integrity of collected field samples.
Upon receipt in the laboratory perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 11.1.2.
Flag data that differs between the two approaches by more than 5% RPD and indicate leakage is suspected or invalidate the data with a similar explanation.
Provide COC information.
Perform gas dilution system evaluations by generating and analyzing blank samples using humidified zero air.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis.
Assess the results from the lowest calibration concentration canister sample and determine if it meets accuracy criteria of ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
In all cases where method modifications are implemented, the analyst must perform the procedures outlined in the IDC (Section. 9.2.3) and verify that all QC acceptance criteria are met.
Record and archive results in tables with associated QC narratives demonstrating acceptable performance criteria. IDC results must be made available upon request.
Perform LB analysis.
Prepare and analyze an LB at the beginning of every sample sequence.
LB/artifact levels must meet the requirements in Table OTM-50-4. If the LB does not meet requirements, perform corrective actions, and re-analyze an LB to ensure QC requirements are met.

Analyze the LB using the same concentrator instrument settings and GC/MS operating conditions as used for the ICAL and field samples. The blank volume used for analysis must match the nominal volume of sample to be analyzed.
Selection of internal standards (IS).

Internal standard(s) (IS) are used to check daily calibration responses. Use standard gaseous mixtures with certified concentrations of IS compounds in ultrapure nitrogen. IS compounds chosen should represent the molecular weight range of the desired target compounds and may be isotopically labelled analogs of target analytes or VOCs that are not expected to be found in field samples.
If using purchased IS stock gases, evaluate the IS upon receipt for the presence of contaminants that may interfere with the quantitation of target compounds.
Prepare a humidified CCV standard in a canister at a concentration in the lower third of the calibration curve.
Analyze a CCV standard to verify the ICAL for each target compound.
Analyze a daily CCV at the beginning of each analytical sequence.
Take corrective action such as instrument maintenance, repreparation of the CCV, and recalibration if ±20% agreement is not met.
Ensure the daily CCV VFC RRFs agree with the ICAL VFC average RRF within ±20% relative percent difference.
Analyze a CCV at the end of the analytical sequence and after every ten samples, whichever is more frequent.
Perform corrective action and reanalyze all samples not bracketed by a passing CCV for the failing compound(s) if sequence continuing or ending CCV fails to meet the criterion.
Include analysis of one replicate field sample in each analytical sequence.
Ensure that target compounds with concentrations >3x the MDL agree within ±25% RPD in the replicate field samples.
Analyze one canister from every cleaning batch as an LB to ensure that the cleaning procedure is adequately removing contaminants that may interfere with quantitation.
Use a canister cleaning apparatus with features equivalent to Figure OTM-50-4 for canister cleaning.
Prepare calibration gas standards using dynamic gas dilution systems or static dilution systems.
Store standards prepared in canisters at ambient laboratory conditions in locations that are free of potential contaminants for a period not to exceed 30 days.
Prepare the VFC calibration standards using procedures in Sections 10.1-10.3.
Prepare analytical sequence for instrument calibration.
Run one LB prior to the calibration standards.
Establish the ICAL using individual standards in separate canisters at each level of the calibration curve and injecting the same volume from each canister.
Run calibration standards from the lowest to highest concentration.
Analyze a LB after the highest calibration standard.
Run a CCV after the LB.
Review all retention times, chromatographic peak shapes, and integrations for the calibration standard compounds to ensure proper quantification of VFCs.
Generate a calibration curve for each VFC target compound.
Analyze seven replicate injections of a canister spiked at a midpoint of the calibration curve.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, repair and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the standard preparation system.
Check for instrument leaks and reanalyze.
Prepare fresh standard and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the chromatographic system, repair and reanalyze.
Flag any sample data that does not meet IS retention time criteria.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, perform maintenance and reanalyze.
Retune and recalibrate instrument and reanalyze.
Flag any sample results with failing IS responses.
Reanalyze all samples after the last passing CCV.
Prepare new CCV at least every 30 days.
Re-prep and reanalyze CCV.
Perform instrument maintenance and reanalyze.
Clean the MS source and rerun the ICAL.
Flag target analytes that do not meet ending CCV criteria.
Run one duplicate field sample analysis in each sample sequence.
Flag the field sample and target analytes that did not meet acceptance criteria.
Analyze one or more cleaned canister as an LB from a given batch of clean canisters.
Analyze other cans in batch as LBs.
Check canister cleaning system for contamination.
Reclean failed canisters.
Verify the amount of CO2 present in the samples before analysis of field samples.
Optimize the analytical system to reduce/eliminate bias, recalibrate, and reanalyze.
Flag any sample results generated using a system that exceeds the ±30 percent bias threshold.

preconditions.text: If the source is a combustion source, ensure moisture content is considered >3% v/v.
If the source is a non-combustion source, determine if acid gases are suspected or present.
If concentrations are >3x MDL, they must be within ±25% of one another.
Sample analytes must be <3 x MDL or <50% of project-required reporting limit, whichever is higher.

constraints.text: The CCV standard must contain all compounds in the calibration mixture.
Recovery of each target compound in the CCV standard must be within ±20% of the theoretical concentration.
The difference between the quantitated concentrations of the target compounds (%Dccv) and the theoretical concentrations should be within ±20%.
Average % recovery of seven replicates must be within ±30% of the concentration prepared in 9.2.3.5.
The lowest calibration concentration must be ≥3x MDL. Must be within ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
Retention time must be within +/-5 seconds of the most recent calibration.
All IS responses must be within ±40% of the average response of the ICAL or most recent calibration.
